title: Spectra of Large Random Stochastic Matrices and relaxation in complex systems
speaker: Reimer Kuehn

proof_stages.label: Beta-ensemble model and universality targets
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the beta matrix ensemble, correlation functions, linear statistics, gap probabilities, and generating functionals, and frames the goal as transferring global and local universality statements from the Gaussian case to general potentials.

proof_stages.label: Equilibrium measure and input estimates
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the logarithmic-energy equilibrium measure, generic one-cut conditions, convergence of integrated correlation functions, Johansson’s small-perturbation formula, and a large-deviation reduction allowing all integrations to be restricted to a neighborhood of the limiting spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Global change of variables to Gaussian coordinates
proof_stages.summary: A non-infinitesimal smooth change of variables is applied to the ensemble, producing a transformed Hamiltonian with Gaussian potential, standard logarithmic interaction, a smooth pair-interaction kernel, and a controlled linear term after choosing the map zeta to transport the equilibrium density to the semicircle law.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian linearization of the pair interaction
proof_stages.summary: The smooth pair interaction is expanded as a difference of positive kernels and linearized by a Hubbard-Stratonovich/Gaussian integral, so partition-function ratios and global generating functionals reduce to Gaussian beta-ensemble perturbation formulas whose convergence is governed by a quadratic form.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of local observables to Gaussian perturbations
proof_stages.summary: For microscopic gap and correlation observables, the same change of variables transforms the scaled intervals correctly up to negligible errors, reducing universality for a general potential to showing that a small perturbation field does not change the corresponding Gaussian local expectation.

proof_stages.label: Removal of the small perturbation
proof_stages.summary: A second near-identity change of variables is built for the Gaussian potential plus perturbation after subtracting a quadratic correction that preserves the support constraints; the remaining interaction is shown negligible using convexity/Brascamp-Lieb estimates, a Valko-Virag shift estimate removes the quadratic term for gap probabilities, and a complex-analytic two-constants argument extends bounds from real to complex fields.

proof_stages.label: One-cut and multi-cut consequences
proof_stages.summary: The resulting theorems give one-cut universality of gap probabilities, generating functions, and correlation-function limits under smoothness assumptions, extend the method to analytic multi-cut potentials using previous reductions, and compare the strength of the averaging with earlier results of Bourgade-Erdos-Yau-type and Beckerman-type works.

examples.name: Classical beta values as matrix ensembles
examples.note: The cases beta=1,2,4 are mentioned only to explain why the beta ensemble is called a matrix model: they correspond to real symmetric, Hermitian, and symplectic random matrices.
examples.engagement_reason: The beta values 1, 2, and 4 are cited as corresponding to real symmetric, Hermitian, and symplectic ensembles, with no computation or construction carried out on those cases.

passes.reason: The values β=1,2,4 are the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant symmetry classes, used only to relate β-ensembles to matrix models.
The values β=1,2,4 are the classical invariant matrix-ensemble cases rather than sporadic exceptions or adversarial cases.
The values beta=1,2,4 are the classical invariant matrix-ensemble cases with orthogonal/unitary/symplectic symmetry, used only to identify familiar realizations of the beta ensemble.
β=1,2,4 are the Dyson classes realized by highly invariant orthogonal/unitary/symplectic matrix ensembles, used only as motivating cases.
The values beta=1,2,4 are the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant matrix symmetry classes, used only to motivate the model.
The β=1,2,4 cases are the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant symmetry classes, invoked as matrix realizations.
The beta values 1, 2, and 4 are used as the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant matrix realizations, not to refute a claim.
The beta values 1,2,4 are the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant matrix-ensemble cases, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
β=1,2,4 are the orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant matrix-ensemble cases, used to motivate the model rather than refute a claim.
The beta values 1,2,4 are used to illustrate the classical invariant matrix ensembles rather than refute a claim, and their special feature is classical group symmetry.
The beta values 1, 2, and 4 are the classical orthogonal/unitary/symplectic invariant matrix-ensemble cases, distinguished by their group symmetries rather than by failure of a claim.
the beta values 1,2,4 are used to identify the classical invariant matrix symmetry classes, not to refute a claim

examples.name: Indicator test function counting eigenvalues in an interval
examples.note: Taking the test function h to be the indicator of an interval makes the linear eigenvalue statistic equal to the number of eigenvalues in that interval, making the general definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the linear eigenvalue statistic to the interval indicator and explicitly identifies the resulting sum as the eigenvalue count in the interval.

passes.reason: The interval indicator is an ordinary bounded step test function used to make a linear eigenvalue statistic count eigenvalues.
The interval-indicator test function is an ordinary linear statistic used to count eigenvalues in an interval.
The interval indicator is an ordinary bounded test function used to produce the eigenvalue-counting linear statistic, not to refute any claim.
An interval indicator is an ordinary special test function for a linear eigenvalue statistic, not used to refute anything.
The interval indicator is an ordinary special case of a linear eigenvalue statistic used to count eigenvalues, not to refute a claim.
The interval indicator is a standard bounded test function used to produce an eigenvalue counting statistic.
The interval indicator is a sharp 0–1, nonsmooth test function specializing a linear statistic to a count, and is used illustratively.
The interval indicator is an ordinary test function for a linear eigenvalue statistic, used simply to count eigenvalues in an interval.
The interval indicator is a standard linear eigenvalue statistic for counting points, introduced definitionally rather than as a limiting or adversarial case.
The interval indicator is presented as a standard linear eigenvalue statistic counting eigenvalues, with no extremal or obstructive role.
The interval indicator is used as an ordinary special test function producing the eigenvalue count, not as an obstruction or structurally extreme object.
the interval indicator is an ordinary test function used to illustrate a linear eigenvalue statistic counting eigenvalues

examples.name: Empty-interval gap event
examples.note: The speaker writes the m=0 indicator for the event that an interval contains no eigenvalues as a simple special case of the more general count-in-intervals probabilities.
examples.engagement_reason: The empty-interval gap event is expressed via its indicator and then used through transformations and integrals in the universality argument.

passes.reason: The gap event is the zero-count, minimum-occupancy case of the interval-counting events, studied for universality rather than as a refutation.
The gap event is the zero-count case for an interval, hence empty/minimal and at the lower boundary of possible counts, but not used against a claim.
The gap event is the zero-count case for an interval, hence an empty/minimal boundary case in the family of eigenvalue-count events, used as a target of universality rather than a counterexample.
The gap event is the zero-occupancy boundary case among interval-counting events, used as a universality observable rather than a counterexample.
The empty-interval gap event is the zero-count, minimum-occupancy case for an interval in the point process.
The gap event is the zero-eigenvalue, minimal-occupancy event for an interval.
The gap event is the zero-occupancy case for an interval, the minimal and empty case among eigenvalue-count events, not a counterexample.
The gap event is the zero-count occupancy case for an interval, a minimal empty case used to define gap probabilities rather than refute a claim.
The gap event is the zero-count event N(Δ)=0, the empty/minimal occupancy case used to define gap probabilities, not to disprove anything.
The empty-gap event is used as a basic gap-probability observable, and structurally it is the zero-count/minimum-occupancy case for an interval.
The empty-interval gap event is the zero-count/minimal occupancy case for an interval, used to define gap probabilities rather than to refute anything.
the gap event is the minimal zero-count/empty-configuration case for eigenvalues in an interval, used as a universality observable
